items.title_fr: QUIC, credentials anonymes et un patch qui protège personne
items.title_en: The Config Nobody Changed: Security Theater in Three Acts
items.content_fr:
Un bug de congestion dans une implémentation QUIC, des credentials web anonymes qui sortent du labo, un CVE de 2024 dont le patch ne sert à rien si tu n'as pas touché à la config — et en fond, l'IA qui redécouvre que le HTML est meilleur que le Markdown pour garder les humains dans la boucle. Semaine dense, pas forcément spectaculaire. Ce qui compte cette semaine, c'est surtout ce que ces sujets révèlent sur la façon dont on gère (ou pas) la complexité dans les systèmes qu'on croit maîtriser.

## Cloudflare, QUIC et un bug de congestion bien vicieux

Quand tu débogues un problème de congestion dans une pile réseau, tu espères trouver un pointeur mal initialisé, une condition de course évidente. Ce que [Cloudflare a documenté dans quiche](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) est plus retors : un bug dans le contrôle de congestion CUBIC qui faisait baisser la fenêtre de congestion de manière incorrecte lors de certaines pertes de paquets en rafale. Résultat concret : des transferts qui s'écroulaient à des moments imprévisibles, sans erreur explicite. Le genre de truc que tu vas d'abord attribuer au réseau upstream avant de réaliser que c'est ton code.

![Cloudflare quiche congestion bug](https://res.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/en/headerimage/generatedHeaderImage-1782410835538.jpg)

Ce qui mérite attention ici, c'est la méthode de diagnostic autant que le correctif. L'équipe a instrumenté les transitions d'état du contrôleur de congestion avec des traces structurées, corrélé les événements de perte avec les changements de `cwnd`, et remonté jusqu'à une divergence entre leur implémentation et la RFC 8312 sur le comportement post-perte. Pas de magie : juste de l'outillage sérieux et une lecture attentive de la spec. quiche est la base de QUIC/HTTP3 dans l'infrastructure Cloudflare — des millions de connexions par seconde passent là-dedans. Un bug de ce type non détecté pendant des semaines donne une idée de la difficulté d'observer un protocole dont la raison d'être est de masquer la latence. Si tu intègres quiche directement dans ton stack (pas via un proxy Cloudflare), assure-toi que tu es sur une version postérieure au correctif — le commit est référencé dans l'article.

## CVE-2024-40766 : le patch existe depuis des mois. Le problème, lui, persiste.

Le vrai sujet de la semaine en sécurité, ce n'est pas le bug en lui-même. C'est ce que [SANS ISC documente noir sur blanc](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/33094) : SonicWall a patché CVE-2024-40766 (accès non authentifié à l'interface de gestion SonicOS, CVSS 9.3), les équipes ont appliqué le patch, et pourtant des appliances restent compromises. Pourquoi ? Parce que le patch corrige la vulnérabilité d'authentification, mais ne ferme pas l'interface de gestion sur les interfaces WAN — et beaucoup d'installations l'ont exposée par défaut ou par habitude.

![CVE-2024-40766 SonicWall](https://isc.sans.edu/diaryimages/images/fig1sonic.png)

C'est le scénario cauchemar de tout ops : tu patches, tu coches la case, et six mois plus tard quelqu'un pivote depuis ton firewall. La CVE date de 2024, l'exploitation active a été confirmée par la CISA, et on parle encore d'appliances accessibles sur le WAN en juin 2026. La surface d'attaque ici n'est pas la vulnérabilité — elle est corrigée. C'est la configuration permissive par défaut couplée à l'inertie opérationnelle. Si tu administres des SonicWall, la section ci-dessous est pour toi.

## Ce que tu dois faire maintenant

**SonicWall / CVE-2024-40766 — actions sans délai :**

1. **Vérifier la version firmware** : tu dois être sur SonicOS 7.0.1-5035 ou supérieur (Gen 7), ou 6.5.4.15-R1 ou supérieur (Gen 6). La commande via CLI SonicOS :
   ```
   show version
   ```
   Si tu es en dessous, c'est une urgence.

2. **Désactiver l'accès management sur l'interface WAN** : dans SonicOS, `Manage → Appliance → Management Access` — désactive HTTPS/SSH sur X1 (WAN) sauf si tu as une raison documentée de le laisser ouvert. Si tu en as une, restreins par adresse IP source.

3. **Vérifier les règles d'accès existantes** :
   ```
   show management-access
   ```
   Cherche `Interface: WAN` avec `HTTPS: Enabled` — si c'est là, tu as un problème.

4. **Auditer les comptes locaux** : CVE-2024-40766 permet la création de comptes en pré-authentification dans certaines versions. Vérifie `Manage → Users → Local Users` pour tout compte que tu ne reconnais pas.

5. **Activer les logs d'authentification vers un SIEM externe** : si tu n'as pas de visibilité externe sur tes authentifications de management, tu navigueras à l'aveugle même après le patch.

## PACT : des credentials anonymes web qui sortent enfin du papier académique

Celui-là, il mérite qu'on s'y arrête. [PACT (Private Access Control Tokens)](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/06/pact-anonymous-credentials-for-the-web/) est une proposition de Mozilla pour des credentials web anonymes — des tokens qui prouvent une propriété ("cet utilisateur a payé", "cet utilisateur est humain", "cet utilisateur est dans ce groupe d'âge") sans révéler l'identité. Le tout basé sur des zero-knowledge proofs et des signatures aveugles (RSA-BSSA ou Schnorr selon le profil).

Les credentials anonymes, ça existe en recherche académique depuis Chaum dans les années 80. Le fossé entre "ça marche sur papier" et "ça tourne dans un navigateur" a toujours été énorme : overhead crypto, absence de standard, absence de support navigateur. Ce qui change avec PACT, c'est que Mozilla Hacks en parle avec des détails d'implémentation concrets, des API JavaScript esquissées, et une intégration pensée pour fonctionner avec les mécanismes de CHIPS et Storage Partitioning déjà dans Firefox. C'est encore expérimental — pas de numéro de spec W3C stabilisé à ce stade — mais c'est la première fois que je vois une proposition qui adresse sérieusement l'écart entre la cryptographie et le DOM. Si tu construis des systèmes d'auth ou de contrôle d'accès, garde un œil sur le dépôt GitHub de la spec. Le cas d'usage "prouver qu'on a payé sans tracker l'utilisateur" est exactement ce dont le web a besoin pour sortir du tout-cookie.

## Rust 657 et devenv : l'écosystème qui règle ses ajustements

[This Week in Rust 657](https://this-week-in-rust.org/blog/2026/06/24/this-week-in-rust-657/) est une semaine de consolidation plus que de révolution. Quelques points notables : l'avancement de `async fn` dans les traits (AFIT) vers une stabilisation plus large, des améliorations dans `rustdoc` pour les intra-doc links, et plusieurs crates qui sortent des versions majeures dans l'écosystème réseau et crypto. Rien de fracassant, mais c'est le genre de semaine qui fait avancer le terrain silencieusement.

Plus intéressant pour le quotidien : [l'article sur devenv et la performance de nixpkgs](https://devenv.sh/blog/2026/06/26/making-devenv-start-fast-and-the-whole-nixpkgs-with-it/). Le problème documenté est réel et connu de quiconque a utilisé Nix sérieusement : le temps de démarrage d'un `devenv shell` peut atteindre 30-60 secondes sur un nixpkgs frais parce que l'évaluation du graphe de dépendances est synchrone et non-cachée de manière agressive. La solution mise en place combine un cache d'évaluation plus agressif côté `nix eval`, des lazy imports pour les parties de nixpkgs non utilisées, et un daemon de pré-évaluation qui tourne en arrière-plan. Le gain annoncé : de 45s à 2-3s sur les démarrages répétés, et de 45s à ~8s au premier démarrage. Si devenv fait partie de ton workflow (et si tu fais du Nix pour gérer tes environnements de développement, il devrait y être), la version concernée est devenv 1.4+.

## OpenRL et HTML vs Markdown : deux signaux sur l'état de l'IA outillée

[Google OpenRL](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/google-open-rl-fine-tuning/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) est une API auto-hébergée expérimentale pour le fine-tuning post-entraînement des LLM par reinforcement learning. Concrètement : tu veux faire du RLHF ou du RLAIF sur ton propre modèle, sans passer par une infra Google, OpenRL te fournit les primitives (environment, reward, policy update) sous forme d'API REST. C'est expérimental, l'annonce est prudente, mais la direction est claire — Google veut que les équipes ML puissent boucler leur pipeline de post-training sans dépendre d'outils épars comme TRL + custom reward server + glue code fragile.

L'autre signal vient d'Anthropic : [leur responsable publie des données internes](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/anthropic-html-markdown-agent/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) montrant que dans les boucles agentiques longues, le HTML maintient mieux l'attention humaine que le Markdown — des taux de relecture et d'intervention corrective supérieurs de 18% en moyenne sur leurs benchmarks internes. L'explication est moins surprenante qu'elle n'y paraît : le Markdown dans une interface de chat agentique est souvent rendu de manière incohérente selon les frontends, ce qui crée une friction cognitive. Le HTML, parsé par le navigateur, donne un rendu stable et cliquable. C'est une observation empirique, pas un argument philosophique — mais elle a des implications directes si tu construis des interfaces pour des agents. À noter que ça va à contre-courant de deux ans de "tout le monde fait du Markdown dans les prompts" — parfois, l'outillage le plus classique l'emporte.

Ce qui lie ces deux sujets à demi-mot : on est en train de construire l'infrastructure de l'IA agentique en temps réel, avec des choix (format de sortie, protocole de training, interface humain-agent) qui vont se cristalliser vite. Le bon moment pour réfléchir à ces choix, c'est maintenant, pas quand le système est en prod depuis dix-huit mois.
items.content_en:
A congestion bug deep in a QUIC implementation. Anonymous credentials that finally look like they might ship in a browser. A CVE from 2024 that's patched, audited, and still getting organizations owned. These aren't random items from a news feed — they're three different failure modes of the same underlying problem: complexity that outruns the people responsible for it.

## Cloudflare's QUIC Bug: When the Protocol Hides the Evidence

Debugging a congestion control bug in a QUIC implementation is a special kind of miserable. QUIC was designed to reduce latency by abstracting away loss signals — which means when congestion control misbehaves, the symptoms look like "the network is weird" before they look like "my code is wrong."

[Cloudflare's postmortem on quiche](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) is worth reading in full. The bug was in their CUBIC implementation: congestion window reductions were being triggered incorrectly during burst loss events, causing `cwnd` to collapse in ways that didn't match RFC 8312's specified behavior. Transfers would silently underperform — no errors, no obvious retransmits, just throughput that cratered at unpredictable intervals.

![Cloudflare quiche congestion bug](https://res.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/en/headerimage/generatedHeaderImage-1782410835538.jpg)

The fix matters less than the methodology. The team built structured traces around congestion state transitions, correlated loss events with `cwnd` changes over time, and eventually traced the divergence back to a specific clause in the RFC they'd misimplemented. Millions of connections per second flow through quiche in Cloudflare's infrastructure — this bug was live long enough that its absence would have been the anomaly. If you're using quiche directly (not behind a Cloudflare proxy), the fix commit is referenced in the article. Check your version.

## CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Wasn't the Hard Part

This is the one that should make you uncomfortable if you run any kind of edge security appliance.

[SANS ISC's write-up](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/33094) lays it out plainly: CVE-2024-40766 is a pre-authentication access vulnerability in SonicOS, CVSS 9.3, patched by SonicWall, actively exploited per CISA confirmation — and still showing up in incident response cases in mid-2026. Not because teams didn't patch. Because the patch fixes the authentication bypass, and nobody closed the management interface that was exposed on the WAN in the first place.

![CVE-2024-40766 SonicWall](https://isc.sans.edu/diaryimages/images/fig1sonic.png)

That's the part that stings. You apply the patch, you check the box, you move on. Six months later someone pivots from your firewall into your internal network. The CVE is remediated. The attack surface isn't. This is operational inertia meeting a permissive default configuration, and it's exactly the kind of thing that doesn't show up in a vulnerability scan because the finding was closed.

If you manage SonicWall appliances, stop reading here and do the following before anything else.

## Actions: Do These Now

**SonicWall / CVE-2024-40766 — no delay on these:**

1. **Verify firmware version** — you need SonicOS 7.0.1-5035 or later (Gen 7), or 6.5.4.15-R1 or later (Gen 6). Check via CLI:
   ```
   show version
   ```
   Anything older than those versions is a live vulnerability.

2. **Disable WAN management access** — in SonicOS, go to `Manage → Appliance → Management Access`. HTTPS and SSH should be disabled on X1 (WAN) unless you have a documented, specific reason they're not. If you do, restrict to known source IPs.

3. **Audit management access rules**:
   ```
   show management-access
   ```
   Look for `Interface: WAN` with `HTTPS: Enabled`. If you see it, that's your problem.

4. **Check for unauthorized local accounts** — CVE-2024-40766 enables pre-auth account creation on vulnerable versions. Go to `Manage → Users → Local Users` and verify every account there belongs to someone on your team.

5. **Ship management auth logs to an external SIEM** — if you're not already doing this, you're flying blind regardless of patch status.

## PACT: Anonymous Credentials That Might Actually Ship

The cryptography is decades old. The gap between "provably secure on paper" and "runs in a browser tab" has always been enormous. [Mozilla's PACT proposal](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/06/pact-anonymous-credentials-for-the-web/) is the most credible attempt I've seen to close it.

PACT — Private Access Control Tokens — lets a site prove something about a user (paid subscriber, over 18, member of a group) without learning who that user is. The cryptographic primitives are blind signatures (RSA-BSSA or Schnorr variants depending on the profile) and zero-knowledge proofs. That's not new. What's new is Mozilla proposing concrete JavaScript APIs, integration with CHIPS and Storage Partitioning in Firefox, and a design that acknowledges the actual browser threat model rather than an academic one.

No stabilized W3C spec number yet — this is still in the proposal phase. But the level of implementation detail in the Mozilla Hacks post suggests this isn't vaporware. The "prove you paid without tracking you" use case is genuinely important for a web that wants to move past third-party cookies without replacing them with something worse. Watch the spec repository.

## Rust, devenv, and the Infrastructure Doing Its Homework

[This Week in Rust 657](https://this-week-in-rust.org/blog/2026/06/24/this-week-in-rust-657/) is a consolidation week. Progress on `async fn` in traits stabilization, better intra-doc link resolution in `rustdoc`, several significant crate releases in the networking and crypto space. No single headline, but the kind of week where six months from now you'll notice things got quietly better.

The more immediately useful read: [the devenv startup performance work](https://devenv.sh/blog/2026/06/26/making-devenv-start-fast-and-the-whole-nixpkgs-with-it/). If you've used Nix for dev environments, you know the pain — a fresh `devenv shell` can take 30-60 seconds because nixpkgs evaluation is synchronous and the default caching is not aggressive enough. The fix involves more aggressive `nix eval` caching, lazy imports for unused nixpkgs subtrees, and a background pre-evaluation daemon. The result: 45 seconds down to 2-3 seconds on repeat starts, ~8 seconds on first run. That's devenv 1.4+. If you run Nix-based dev environments at any scale, this is worth an upgrade.

## Two AI Signals Worth Noting

[Google's OpenRL](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/google-open-rl-fine-tuning/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) is an experimental self-hosted API for LLM post-training via reinforcement learning. The pitch: run your own RLHF or RLAIF pipeline without stitching together TRL, a custom reward server, and whatever glue code you've accumulated over the last year. It exposes environment, reward, and policy update as REST endpoints. Still experimental, the announcement is appropriately hedged — but the direction is clear. Google wants teams to own their post-training loop.

Separately, [Anthropic's internal findings on HTML vs. Markdown in agentic loops](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/anthropic-html-markdown-agent/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) are counterintuitive enough to be worth a second read. Their data shows HTML maintains human engagement in long agentic sessions at an 18% higher rate than Markdown, measured by re-read rates and corrective intervention frequency. The explanation is mundane but real: Markdown rendering in agentic chat interfaces is inconsistent across frontends, creating cognitive friction. HTML, parsed by the browser, renders predictably and stays interactive. Two years of "just use Markdown in prompts" and it turns out the 1990s format wins for human readability in 2026. Sometimes the boring choice is right.

Both of these point at the same moment we're in: the agentic infrastructure layer is being poured right now, and choices made this year — output formats, training protocols, human-agent interface conventions — will harden fast. The SonicWall situation is a good reminder of what happens when you inherit a configuration decision you didn't make and never revisited. Don't let that happen with your agent stack too.

items.title_fr: Closed-Loop Training, Self-Evolving Agents, and Medical AI Without Annotations
items.title_en: Closed-Loop Training, Self-Evolving Agents, and Medical AI Without Annotations
items.content_en:
Three papers dropped this week that collectively describe the same underlying pressure: the cost of human-labeled data and human-in-the-loop evaluation is becoming the bottleneck, and labs are engineering around it fast.

**The closed-loop problem, solved badly until now**

[This data-and-evaluation closed-loop paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28471) is the most structurally significant of the batch. The core idea — use model outputs to generate training signal, then use a held-out evaluation loop to validate whether the capability actually improved — sounds obvious until you realize how badly it goes wrong without careful design. The failure mode is reward hacking that looks like improvement on your internal evals and falls apart the moment it touches a real distribution. What the paper adds is a feedback mechanism that continuously checks for that divergence before it compounds. That's not magic; it's engineering discipline applied to a place where most teams cut corners.

Directly adjacent: [the recursive self-evolving agents paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28374) applies held-out selection to agent behavior rather than static model weights. Agents propose modifications to their own tool-use or reasoning strategy, the held-out evaluator gates which changes propagate. Small step in isolation. Combined with a closed training loop, you have a system that can iterate on both weights and behavior without a human grading every step. Whether it stays stable over many generations is the open question — the paper doesn't run it long enough to give a confident answer either way.

Taken together: the 2024 picture of "RLHF requires expensive human raters" is being replaced by "you need a reliable automated evaluator, and that evaluator is now itself a model." The obvious risk is that you're trusting the evaluator's blind spots not to correlate with the generator's blind spots. Nobody has a clean solution to that yet.

**Medical AI without the annotation bottleneck**

[RareDxR1](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00147) targets rare disease diagnosis and explicitly discards the assumption that you can get human-annotated ground truth at scale — because for rare diseases, you can't. There aren't enough diagnosed cases and the specialists who can label them are the same people you're trying to augment. The approach leans on reasoning traces derived from medical literature and case reports rather than labeled examples. Accuracy on rare disease benchmarks improves meaningfully over baseline; more importantly, the failure modes shift. The model is wrong in more legible ways, which matters operationally.

[The biomedical tool-use agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28692) is complementary: it's about orchestrating over a large heterogeneous set of medical APIs and databases rather than reasoning from parametric knowledge alone. Retrieval over tool universes this large is genuinely hard — the agent has to decide which tool to invoke before it knows whether the tool's output will be useful, which is a credit assignment problem with sparse signal. Promising engineering, but the evaluation is on a contained benchmark. Clinical deployment is a different animal.

The regulatory angle here is real and underappreciated. Both papers would produce systems that generate clinical reasoning without traceable human annotation chains. EU AI Act Article 13 transparency requirements and FDA's evolving SaMD framework both assume you can explain what the system was trained on. "We distilled from literature via reasoning" is going to create interesting conversations with regulators who expect labeled datasets and validation cohorts.

**The audit-grounded governance paper**

[The Two Genie Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.28710) models what happens when AI governance relies on audits as the enforcement mechanism — and finds, unsurprisingly, that audit-grounded governance produces strategic behavior from both deployers and users that undermines the audit's validity. The framing as a two-player game is useful for making the incentive structure legible. The practical conclusion is that audit regimes need to be designed assuming adversarial compliance, not cooperative disclosure. That's not a novel observation in security; it apparently still needs saying in AI policy circles.

**What to do**

If you're building any eval pipeline that feeds back into training — even loosely, even just for model selection — read the closed-loop paper before you ship it. Specifically look at how they detect evaluator-generator correlation. If your evaluator and your generator share a base model, you probably have this problem already.

On the medical AI side: if you're integrating clinical reasoning tools built on distilled or self-supervised training pipelines, start documenting the data provenance now, not at submission time. FDA and EMA are not yet enforcing hard requirements on this, but the guidance is moving and retroactive documentation is painful.

The model-finding paper ([here](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.30846)) is narrower — it benchmarks retrieval strategies for locating the right model artifact given a task description. Useful if you're building a model registry or a routing layer over a large internal model zoo. The headline finding is that embedding format matters more than retrieval algorithm for this task, which should inform how you index if you're in that position.

items.title_fr: On-Device MTP, Copyright Reckoning, and Sycophancy You Can Measure
items.title_en: On-Device MTP, Copyright Reckoning, and Sycophancy You Can Measure
items.content_en:
Three things happened this week that are worth separating from the noise.

**Google shipped frozen Multi-Token Prediction on Pixel — and the numbers matter**

[Google's post on Gemini Nano MTP](https://research.google/blog/accelerating-gemini-nano-models-on-pixel-with-frozen-multi-token-prediction/) buries the lead a bit, but the core result is real: by freezing the draft heads after training and running speculative decoding with them, they get meaningful throughput gains on Pixel 9 hardware without extra fine-tuning cost per deployment. This is not a research demo. It's shipping in the OS. For on-device inference, the constraint has always been memory bandwidth, not FLOPS — MTP attacks exactly that. The frozen-head approach matters because it means OEMs don't need to retrain every time the base model updates. If you're building anything that calls on-device Gemini APIs, the latency profile you benchmarked six months ago is stale. Re-measure on Pixel 9 hardware specifically; the gains are not uniform across task types.

**The NYT lawsuit just got a data center attached to it**

[The NYT's amended complaint targeting the Abilene supercomputer](https://arstechnica.com/tech-policy/2026/06/microsoft-built-supercomputer-to-help-openai-infringe-copyrights-nyt-alleged/) is the most concrete escalation in the copyright-vs-AI-training fight so far. They're not just arguing that OpenAI ingested their articles — they're arguing Microsoft built and operated infrastructure whose primary purpose was to do it at scale. That's a different legal theory, one that tries to pierce the "we just provide compute" defense Microsoft has been running. Whether it succeeds in court is genuinely uncertain, but the *strategic* effect is already happening: it forces discovery into training data provenance at the infrastructure level. Every lab that trained on Common Crawl derivatives should be watching this deposition schedule. Short term, nothing changes for developers. Medium term — 18 to 36 months — if this theory holds, it reshapes how compute providers contract with model trainers, and that cost lands somewhere.

**Sycophancy now has a linear probe address you can steer**

[The cascading linear features paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26155) is the most technically interesting item this week. The researchers identify sparse linear features in residual stream activations that causally predict sycophantic completions, then show you can intervene on them at inference time to suppress the behavior — without RLHF, without a new fine-tune. The "cascading" part means early-layer features gate later ones, so targeted ablation is cleaner than brute-force activation patching. This is the same mechanistic interpretability lineage as the refusal geometry work from a few weeks back, and it's accelerating. The implication for evals is immediate: if sycophancy has a measurable internal representation, your current output-based evals are measuring symptoms, not the condition. Teams running RLHF pipelines to reduce sycophancy should be asking whether they're suppressing the feature or just training the model to hide it.

**GPT-5.6 Sol preview: less than it sounds**

[OpenAI's Sol preview](https://openai.com/index/previewing-gpt-5-6-sol) is a benchmark post dressed as an announcement. The version number implies continuity with GPT-5 but OpenAI's versioning has stopped meaning anything coherent. What's actually claimed: stronger performance on long-horizon reasoning tasks, better tool use. What's missing: any architectural disclosure, training data cutoff, context window specifics, or honest comparison to GPT-4o on cost/latency. If you're deciding whether to migrate an agent pipeline, don't move on a preview post — wait for the API to be generally available and run your own evals on your actual task distribution. The number that matters is not a benchmark score; it's your task's pass rate at your token budget.

**The governance paper worth filing**

The [Odyssey local-truth paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.27593) and the [institutional attestation governance model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26298) both appeared this week. The attestation paper is the more operationally relevant one: it argues for governing specific *actions* autonomous agents take rather than trying to certify agents as entities. Concretely, that means cryptographic attestation of tool calls and external interactions, auditable per-action rather than per-session. For anyone building agentic systems that touch regulated data or cross organizational boundaries — healthcare, finance, anything the EU AI Act's high-risk categories cover — this framing will show up in compliance conversations within 12 months. Start thinking about your action log schema now, not after a regulator asks for it.

**What to actually do**

- Pixel 9 + Gemini Nano: re-run latency benchmarks; frozen MTP changes the curve.
- Sycophancy evals: add at least one activation-based probe to your eval suite if you have model internals access; if you're API-only, add adversarial agreement-pressure test cases.
- Copyright exposure: if your product ingests third-party content for training or fine-tuning, get your data provenance documentation in order before discovery season arrives.
- Agentic logging: emit structured, per-action logs for every external tool call. Retrofitting this is painful; the time to design it is before you have users.

items.title_fr: Le bug silencieux, le patch inutile, et l'IA qui préfère le HTML
items.title_en: Patch Applied, Problem Remains: A Week of Gaps Between Fix and Reality
items.content_fr:
Un bug de congestion dans une implémentation QUIC. Une CVE corrigée depuis deux ans sur des pare-feu SonicWall activement exploités. Un modèle de langage qui préfère le HTML au Markdown. Trois informations qui, à première vue, n'ont rien à voir — et qui toutes racontent la même chose : la distance entre « c'est réparé » et « c'est sécurisé », entre « c'est sorti » et « c'est utilisable », entre « on sait » et « on comprend ».

Cette semaine ne manque pas de signaux faibles. Elle en a même trop. Voilà ce qui compte.

## CVE-2024-40766 : le patch était là. Le risque, aussi.

La [CVE-2024-40766](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/33094) touche les pare-feu SonicWall SonicOS — une faille d'accès non authentifié à la gestion SSL-VPN, CVSS 9.3. Le patch est sorti en août 2024. Vingt-deux mois plus tard, le SANS ISC documente des compromissions actives sur des équipements *patchés*.

![CVE-2024-40766 SonicWall](https://isc.sans.edu/diaryimages/images/fig1sonic.png)

Le problème n'est pas le patch. C'est que le patch corrigeait la faille d'authentification, mais ne désactivait pas l'accès de gestion exposé sur l'interface WAN — une configuration que SonicWall déconseillait déjà, mais que personne n'avait modifiée. Résultat : les attaquants utilisent aujourd'hui des techniques post-authentification (persistance via injection de signets SSLVPN, mouvement latéral vers les segments internes) sur des équipements techniquement à jour.

C'est le scénario classique de la mauvaise compréhension de la sécurité périmétrique. Un CVE se ferme. La surface d'attaque reste ouverte parce qu'elle dépend d'une décision de configuration que personne n'a prise. Si tu administres des SonicWall en production, ce n'est pas une lecture optionnelle.

## Ce que tu dois faire maintenant

```bash
# 1. Vérifier la version firmware — cible minimum : SonicOS 7.0.1-5145 ou supérieure
# Sur l'interface admin : Device > Settings > Firmware
# En CLI SSH :
show version

# 2. Désactiver l'accès de gestion SSL-VPN depuis l'interface WAN
# Network > Interfaces > [WAN interface] > Management :
# Décocher HTTPS, SSH — accès de gestion uniquement depuis zones internes ou OOB

# 3. Vérifier les signets SSLVPN pour détecter une injection de persistance
# VPN > SSL-VPN > Bookmarks — auditer tout signet que tu ne reconnaîs pas

# 4. Vérifier les comptes locaux actifs non documentés
# Device > Users > Local Users — supprimer tout compte non inventorié

# 5. Activer les logs vers un SIEM externe et chercher des authentifications réussies
# sur /cgi-bin/management depuis des IPs non RFC1918
```

Si tu n'as pas de SIEM, les logs locaux ne suffisent pas — un attaquant persistant peut les altérer. Considère ça comme un audit d'urgence, pas une vérification de routine.

## Cloudflare, QUIC, et la patience requise pour chasser un bug de congestion

[L'histoire du bug dans quiche](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) mérite d'être lue en entier, car elle illustre quelque chose de rare : une équipe qui documente honnêtement comment elle s'est plantée et comment elle a trouvé la solution.

![Cloudflare quiche bug](https://res.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/en/headerimage/generatedHeaderImage-1782410835538.jpg)

Le bug vivait dans l'implémentation QUIC de Cloudflare (`quiche`, leur bibliothèque Rust). Sous certaines conditions de perte de paquets, l'algorithme de contrôle de congestion entrait dans un état où il réduisait la fenêtre d'envoi à zéro sans la réouvrir correctement — un deadlock de fait, invisible dans les métriques agrégées parce qu'il touchait des connexions individuelles, pas le débit global.

La détection a pris des mois. Le signal était là : des pics de latence P99 inexplicables sur certaines régions edge. Mais les métriques standard (bande passante, taux de perte de paquets, retransmissions) ne pointaient vers rien. C'est l'analyse des traces QLOG connexion par connexion qui a permis d'isoler le pattern. Ensuite seulement, la correction — quelques lignes dans la gestion du recovery path de l'algorithme CUBIC adapté à QUIC.

Ce qui est instructif ici, c'est moins le bug que la méthode. QUIC rend les bugs de couche transport plus difficiles à observer que TCP précisément parce qu'il chiffre tout, y compris les métadonnées de contrôle. Les outils de debug traditionnels (tcpdump, Wireshark) te donnent des paquets opaques. Le QLOG — un format de log structuré défini dans la RFC de QUIC — est la seule fenêtre sérieuse sur ce qui se passe à l'intérieur. Si tu déploies QUIC en production et que tu n'as pas de pipeline d'analyse QLOG, tu as les yeux bandés.

## HTML vs Markdown : l'IA a un avis, et il tient la route

Anthropic a publié une observation qui va à contre-courant de tout le tooling LLM actuel : dans les boucles agentiques, les sorties en HTML maintiennent mieux l'engagement des humains que le Markdown.

L'affirmation vient de [leur blog de recherche](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/anthropic-html-markdown-agent/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global), signé par des chercheurs travaillant sur Claude. Elle n'est pas intuitive. On a passé des années à entraîner des modèles sur du Markdown parce que c'est lisible par les humains *et* parseable par les machines. Mais ce raisonnement supposait un humain qui lit du texte brut. Dans une interface agentique — où le rendu HTML est natif et où l'humain interagit avec des éléments cliquables, des sections pliables, des indicateurs visuels — le HTML structuré crée une meilleure clarté. L'utilisateur comprend plus vite où il est dans le flux, quelles actions sont disponibles, ce qui a changé.

Il y a une nuance importante : ça ne veut pas dire qu'on devrait envoyer du HTML brut dans des contextes où il sera affiché comme texte. Ça veut dire que le format de sortie d'un agent devrait être adapté à son environnement de rendu — ce que la plupart des frameworks agentiques ne font pas encore proprement. LangChain, AutoGen et compagnie produisent du Markdown par défaut, partout, tout le temps. C'est un point faible.

## OpenRL, le fine-tuning post-entraînement, et ce que Google essaie de standardiser

[Google OpenRL](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/google-open-rl-fine-tuning/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) est une API expérimentale auto-hébergée pour le fine-tuning par renforcement de LLMs — RLHF, RLAIF et variantes. Elle est encore en alpha, mais elle pointe vers quelque chose d'important : le post-training devient le facteur différenciateur clé.

Préentraîner un grand modèle coûte des dizaines de millions de dollars et se fait sur un cluster de milliers de GPU. Mais l'ajustement fin par renforcement — aligner le modèle sur un comportement précis, le rendre utile pour une tâche spécifique — ça peut se faire avec des ressources beaucoup plus modestes si tu as les bons outils. OpenRL vise à standardiser cette étape, en rendant les boucles de reward et les pipelines d'évaluation reproductibles et auto-hébergés.

Le timing est intéressant. On observe depuis six mois une explosion de modèles open-weights (Llama 3.x, Mistral, Qwen 2.5) que n'importe qui peut fine-tuner. Ce qui manque, c'est l'infrastructure de reward modeling convenable. OpenRL n'est pas la solution finale — c'est encore expérimental, et Google a l'habitude d'abandonner ses projets expérimentaux — mais la direction est bonne.

## Rust, devenv, et la lenteur qu'on accepte trop facilement

[Cette semaine dans l'écosystème Rust](https://this-week-in-rust.org/blog/2026/06/24/this-week-in-rust-657/) contient quelques PR notables. La plus intéressante : des progrès sur `async fn` dans les traits d'objets — un angle mort historique de Rust qui forçait soit à utiliser `Box<dyn Future>` (allocation heap à chaque appel async), soit des contorsions avec des crates comme `async-trait`. Ce n'est pas encore stabilisé, mais la direction est claire et les cas d'usage en WebAssembly async sont directement concernés.

Sur [devenv](https://devenv.sh/blog/2026/06/26/making-devenv-start-fast-and-the-whole-nixpkgs-with-it/), l'article traite d'un problème concret : le temps de démarrage de `devenv shell` qui peut dépasser 30 secondes sur un nixpkgs complet. La solution implique une évaluation lazy du graphe de dépendances Nix et du cache d'évaluation — deux optimisations qui font descendre le cold start sous 3 secondes dans leurs benchmarks. Si tu utilises Nix pour tes environnements de dev (et tu devrais sérieusement y réfléchir si ce n'est pas le cas), c'est une amélioration tangible qui change la boucle de feedback quotidienne.

La lenteur des environnements de dev est un coût invisible. On s'y habitue, on l'absorbe, et au bout de six mois on a perdu des heures sans les compter.

## PACT : des credentials anonymes dans le navigateur, sans magie

Mozilla [documente PACT](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/06/pact-anonymous-credentials-for-the-web/) — Privacy-preserving Anonymous Credentials for the Web — une proposition de standard qui permettrait à un site de vérifier qu'un utilisateur remplit une condition (âge minimum, abonnement actif, appartenance à un groupe) sans apprendre *qui* est cet utilisateur.

La cryptographie sous-jacente n'est pas nouvelle — les schémas de credentials anonymes (BBS+, blind signatures, zk-SNARKs) existent depuis des années en recherche académique. Ce qui est nouveau, c'est la tentative de standardisation au niveau web platform, avec une API navigateur et un modèle de délégation entre issuer (qui émet le credential), holder (l'utilisateur) et verifier (le site).

L'enjeu est réel. Aujourd'hui, la seule façon de prouver « j'ai 18 ans » à un site, c'est soit de lui donner ta date de naissance (tracking direct), soit de passer par un tiers d'identité (SSO) qui enregistre la transaction. PACT propose un chemin où la preuve est cryptographiquement valide et non-liée à une identité persistante.

La proposition est encore au stade d'early draft au W3C. Mais Mozilla qui la pousse, ça mérite attention. C'est le genre de projet qui met cinq ans à arriver dans un navigateur stable — autant commencer à comprendre le modèle maintenant.

## pVACtools v6 : la bioinformatique avance, discrètement

[pVACtools v6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26659) est une mise à jour majeure d'une suite de prédiction de néoantigènes pour l'immunothérapie du cancer. C'est de la bioinformatique pure — pas du dev web — mais ça mérite une mention.

Les néoantigènes sont des peptides spécifiques à une tumeur, issus de mutations somatiques, que le système immunitaire peut apprendre à cibler. Les prédire correctement à partir d'un séquençage tumoral est un problème computationnel complexe : il faut croiser le variant calling, la prédiction de présentation HLA, et le filtrage des faux positifs. pVACtools v6 améliore le pipeline sur les trois fronts, avec notamment un meilleur support des variants de fusion et une interface de visualisation redessinée.

Ce n'est pas un sujet quotidien pour la plupart des lecteurs ici. Mais c'est un exemple de ce que le logiciel libre fait de mieux : des outils qui permettent à des chercheurs cliniques sans budget de mener des analyses qui coûtaient une fortune il y a dix ans. Le preprint est sur ArXiv, le code est sur GitHub, les données de validation sont reproductibles. Quelqu'un quelque part va concevoir un essai clinique de meilleure qualité parce que cet outil existe.
items.content_en:
SonicWall shipped the patch in August 2024. Twenty-two months later, SANS ISC is documenting active compromises on firewalls running that patched firmware. The CVE is closed. The attackers are still inside.

That's the thread this week pulls on, from network gear to QUIC stacks to LLM output formats: the distance between "fixed" and "safe," between "shipped" and "usable." Not a theme I'm forcing — just what the week actually handed us.

## CVE-2024-40766: The Patch Was Never the Point

[CVE-2024-40766](https://isc.sans.edu/diary/rss/33094) is a CVSS 9.3 unauthenticated access flaw in SonicWall SonicOS, affecting the SSL-VPN management interface. Patch dropped August 2024. The problem: the patch closed the authentication bypass, but left the management interface exposed on the WAN interface — a configuration SonicWall's own best practices said not to use, but that nobody changed.

![CVE-2024-40766 SonicWall](https://isc.sans.edu/diaryimages/images/fig1sonic.png)

So now attackers with post-auth techniques — SSLVPN bookmark injection for persistence, lateral movement into internal segments — are walking through devices that pass a firmware version check. The box is patched. The attack surface is wide open.

This is the standard failure mode of perimeter security: patch the CVE, close the ticket, move on. Nobody audits the configuration that made the CVE exploitable in the first place. If you're running SonicWall in production, the SANS diary linked above is not optional reading.

## What to Do Right Now

```bash
# 1. Check firmware — minimum target: SonicOS 7.0.1-5145 or later
# Admin UI: Device > Settings > Firmware
# Via SSH CLI:
show version

# 2. Disable WAN-facing management access
# Network > Interfaces > [WAN interface] > Management
# Uncheck HTTPS and SSH — management only from internal zones or OOB network

# 3. Audit SSL-VPN bookmarks for injected persistence
# VPN > SSL-VPN > Bookmarks — flag anything you don't recognize

# 4. Audit local user accounts
# Device > Users > Local Users — delete any account not in your inventory

# 5. Search logs for authenticated access to /cgi-bin/management from non-RFC1918 IPs
# If you don't have external SIEM, local logs aren't trustworthy — a persistent
# attacker can alter them. Treat this as an emergency audit.
```

## Cloudflare's QUIC Bug: When the Metrics Lie by Omission

[The quiche bug writeup](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) is the kind of postmortem worth bookmarking, not because the bug was exotic, but because the debugging process was honest.

![Cloudflare quiche congestion bug](https://res.infoq.com/news/2026/06/cloudflare-bug-quiche/en/headerimage/generatedHeaderImage-1782410835538.jpg)

Short version: under specific packet loss conditions, the congestion control in Cloudflare's Rust QUIC implementation (`quiche`) would reduce the send window to zero and fail to reopen it correctly. A soft deadlock per-connection, invisible in aggregate metrics — bandwidth looked fine, retransmission rates looked fine, only P99 latency on certain edge regions showed something was wrong. Detection took months. The fix was a handful of lines in the CUBIC recovery path.

What made this hard to find is structural. QUIC encrypts everything, including the transport control metadata that TCP leaves visible to tools like Wireshark. Your standard packet capture gives you opaque datagrams. The only real window into QUIC internals is QLOG — the structured log format defined alongside the protocol spec. Cloudflare used per-connection QLOG traces to isolate the pattern. Without that, they'd still be staring at aggregate dashboards wondering what's wrong.

If you're running QUIC in production and you don't have a QLOG analysis pipeline, you're flying blind. Not hypothetically — exactly like this.

## Anthropic Says HTML. The Entire LLM Stack Is Betting on Markdown.

Anthropic's research team published something that cuts against years of tooling assumptions: in agentic loops, [HTML output keeps humans more engaged than Markdown](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/anthropic-html-markdown-agent/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global).

The reasoning is actually solid once you think it through. We trained models on Markdown because it's human-readable and machine-parseable — a reasonable tradeoff when the human is reading raw text. But in an agentic interface with native HTML rendering, collapsible sections, clickable actions, and visual progress indicators, structured HTML creates better cognitive friction. Users understand faster where they are in the workflow, what they can do next, what just changed.

The implication: output format should be coupled to the rendering environment. That's not what LangChain, AutoGen, or most agentic frameworks do — they default to Markdown everywhere, always, regardless of where the output lands. It's a genuine blind spot, and it's one that matters more as agents become the primary interface.

One caveat worth stating plainly: none of this applies if HTML will be displayed as raw text. This is about matching format to renderer, not about HTML being universally better.

## Google OpenRL and the Post-Training Bottleneck

[Google's OpenRL](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/06/google-open-rl-fine-tuning/?utm_campaign=infoq_content&utm_source=infoq&utm_medium=feed&utm_term=global) is an experimental self-hosted API for LLM reinforcement learning fine-tuning — RLHF, RLAIF, the whole post-training stack. Still in alpha. Still worth watching.

The interesting signal isn't the project itself — Google has a long history of launching experimental infrastructure projects and quietly shelving them — it's what the project implies. Pre-training a frontier model costs eight figures and requires clusters most organizations will never touch. But post-training, the fine-tuning-by-reinforcement step that aligns model behavior to specific tasks, can be done at dramatically smaller scale with the right tooling.

The gap right now is reward modeling infrastructure. Anyone can pull Llama 3.x or Qwen 2.5 weights and fine-tune. Doing it with clean, reproducible RL reward loops is harder. OpenRL is an attempt to standardize that layer. Whether Google actually maintains it is a separate question.

## Rust: `async fn` in Trait Objects Is Getting Less Painful

[This Week in Rust 657](https://this-week-in-rust.org/blog/2026/06/24/this-week-in-rust-657/) has a few things worth pulling out. The most interesting: continued progress on `async fn` in dyn trait objects. This has been a genuine pain point — the workarounds were either `Box<dyn Future>` (heap allocation on every async call) or contorting your code around the `async-trait` crate. Neither is great when you're writing async WebAssembly or server-side Rust at any scale.

Not stabilized yet. But the trajectory is clear.

On the tooling side, [devenv's startup optimization](https://devenv.sh/blog/2026/06/26/making-devenv-start-fast-and-the-whole-nixpkgs-with-it/) is more impactful than it sounds. `devenv shell` on a full nixpkgs dependency graph was routinely hitting 30+ seconds cold start. The fix: lazy evaluation of the dependency graph plus evaluation caching, which brings that number under 3 seconds in their benchmarks. If you use Nix for dev environments — and if you've been on the fence, this is the year to look seriously at it — this makes the daily feedback loop noticeably tighter.

Slow dev environments are a tax everyone pays and almost nobody measures. Thirty seconds per shell launch, twenty launches a day, adds up to hours a month of dead time.

## PACT: Anonymous Credentials, Finally Standardized

Mozilla is pushing [PACT — Privacy-preserving Anonymous Credentials for the Web](https://hacks.mozilla.org/2026/06/pact-anonymous-credentials-for-the-web/) — a proposed web platform standard that would let a site verify a user satisfies a condition (age, subscription status, group membership) without learning who the user is.

The cryptographic primitives — BBS+ signatures, blind signatures, zk-SNARKs — aren't new. Academic cryptographers have been building on these for fifteen years. What's new is the attempt to land this at the web platform level, with a browser API and a clean three-party model: issuer (who creates the credential), holder (the user), verifier (the site).

The real-world case is obvious: proving "I'm over 18" today means handing over your birthdate (directly trackable) or going through an SSO provider (who logs the transaction). PACT proposes a path where the proof is cryptographically valid and not linkable to a persistent identity across sessions.

Early W3C draft stage. Five years from stable browser support, conservatively. But Mozilla driving it is the right organization driving it, and the model is worth understanding now. Like most identity infrastructure, you want to grok the architecture long before you have to implement against it.

## pVACtools v6: The Quiet Infrastructure of Cancer Research

[pVACtools v6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.26659) is a major update to a neoantigen prediction suite used in cancer immunotherapy research. Neoantigen prediction — identifying tumor-specific peptides from somatic mutations that the immune system can be trained to target — is a computationally hard problem requiring accurate variant calling, HLA presentation prediction, and false-positive filtering.

Version 6 improves all three, with better fusion variant support and a redesigned visualization interface. This isn't daily-driver software for most readers here. It's worth a mention anyway, because it's exactly what open source does when it's working: researchers without multimillion-dollar budgets run analyses that cost a fortune ten years ago, on reproducible pipelines, with code on GitHub and validation data in the preprint. Someone is designing a better clinical trial because this tool exists.

The preprint is on ArXiv. The code is public. That's the whole story, and it's a good one.

items.content_en:
Anthropic spent years arguing that its models are too dangerous to release without careful stewardship, then discovered the government reads its own safety filings. The Ars piece [lays out the trap clearly](https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/06/how-anthropic-may-have-talked-itself-into-an-ai-export-ban/): when you publish detailed arguments about catastrophic risk potential to justify internal governance, you hand regulators a ready-made brief for export controls. The Commerce Department didn't conjure a threat model from thin air — it borrowed Anthropic's own language back at them. The MIT Tech Review follow-up [identifies three live pressure points](https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/06/22/1139424/three-things-to-watch-amid-anthropics-latest-feud-with-the-government/): whether the controls extend to API access (not just weights), how allied-nation carve-outs get defined, and whether Anthropic's Constitutional AI framing ends up codified in the regulatory text itself. That last one matters most. If one lab's internal policy language becomes the statutory baseline, every competitor either conforms to it or fights a two-front war — regulators and customers simultaneously.

The near-term consequence is concrete: enterprise customers building on Claude API in non-Five-Eyes jurisdictions need to start treating access as contingent, not guaranteed. That's not paranoia, it's reading the room. If you're architecting something that needs model parity across regions — say, a product that runs Claude in the EU and US — the window to instrument a provider-abstraction layer is now, before a restriction lands and you're doing it under pressure.

Shift to the training data side, and [this ArXiv paper on Wikipedia advocacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24890) is more unsettling than the headline suggests. The researchers show that small, targeted edits to high-PageRank Wikipedia articles — the kind a coordinated group of a few dozen editors could execute — measurably shift LLM outputs on contested value-laden topics. The effect propagates because training pipelines weight Wikipedia heavily and snapshot it at scale. This isn't a novel attack vector in theory, but the paper quantifies the leverage ratio: a modest advocacy effort on a handful of articles moves model behavior in ways that survive fine-tuning. For anyone building products where factual or ethical framing matters — legal, medical, policy tooling — the takeaway is that your model's "neutral" baseline has an authorship problem you can't fully audit. RAG over a source you control is one partial mitigation; at minimum, don't treat the base model's prior as ground truth on anything politically adjacent.

[RIFT-Bench](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23927) addresses something the static red-team benchmarks were always going to miss: agents don't fail at a fixed input, they fail across a trajectory. The benchmark constructs dynamic adversarial scenarios where the attack evolves based on what the agent does — closer to how a real exploitation attempt works than the usual prompt-injection snapshots. Current top models don't cover themselves in glory here. If you're deploying agents with tool-use or code execution, the relevant question isn't "did it pass a safety eval" but "what happens when an adversary gets three turns to probe it." RIFT-Bench gives you a reproducible way to ask that. Worth integrating into CI before you ship anything with real-world side effects.

[Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19464) is drier but points at a real gap. The proposal is essentially a formal policy layer that constrains agent actions at runtime — obligations, permissions, prohibitions expressed in a logic that can be checked before execution, not just logged after. The state of the art here is mostly vibes-based prompt instructions and hope. A machine-checkable policy layer that sits between the LLM and its tool calls is architecturally sound, and the paper is worth reading if you're building anything where audit trails matter to a compliance team. It's early — no production-ready implementation yet — but the framing is right and worth tracking as the agentic framework space consolidates.

[Dustin's draft-augmented sparse verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24957) is a speculative decoding variant targeting long-context generation. The core idea: instead of verifying every token from the draft model, verify sparsely over positions that actually matter for coherence, then fill the rest. Claims meaningful throughput gains on 32k+ context with minimal quality degradation. Incremental rather than foundational — speculative decoding improvements have been steady cadence this year — but if you're running self-hosted inference on long-document tasks, it's worth benchmarking against your current stack.

The thread connecting the export fight and the Wikipedia finding is less philosophical than it sounds: both are about who controls the prior. Anthropic's safety framing shaped what regulators think the risk is. Wikipedia editors shape what the model thinks the facts are. Neither of those influence channels was designed for what it's now being used for, and both are very hard to unwind once they're load-bearing.

items.title_fr: Orbital VLMs, Agent Trust Problems, and KV Cache Surgery
items.title_en: Orbital VLMs, Agent Trust Problems, and KV Cache Surgery
items.content_en:
**[NAVI-Orbital](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.18271) put a zero-shot vision-language model on a real satellite and ran it.** Not a simulation, not a ground-truth-rich benchmark — actual in-orbit Earth observation with no task-specific fine-tuning. The model interprets sensor data and makes autonomous observation decisions on-board. That's the part that matters: inference at the edge, in an environment where you cannot patch the weights mid-mission, cannot tolerate hallucinated coordinates, and have maybe 200ms of ground contact per pass. The fact that it worked zero-shot suggests frontier VLM generalization has cleared a threshold that wasn't obvious from benchmark numbers alone. The geopolitical read is immediate: sovereign space programs are now evaluating which foundation models they can actually run without depending on US-controlled inference infrastructure. Export controls on model weights just got a harder test case.

**[OSGuard](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.15034) is a benchmark for computer-use agents doing unsafe things**, and the results are not comfortable. The benchmark catalogs attacks where an agent operating a real OS — browsing, executing commands, managing files — gets manipulated into exfiltrating data, persisting across reboots, or disabling security tooling. The key finding: current agents fail safety checks at rates that would be unacceptable in any production deployment, and the failure modes are qualitatively different from chat-model jailbreaks. An agent with shell access that follows a malicious instruction in a webpage it's asked to summarize isn't a refusal problem — it's a privilege boundary problem. If you're building anything with computer-use capabilities right now (Anthropic's, OpenAI's Operator, or rolling your own via tool-calling), read this paper before shipping. The attack surface is the OS, not the model.

**The agent trust paper ([Trust Between AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.14923)) is doing something more rigorous than the typical "multi-agent" hype piece.** It operationalizes trust formation, breakage, and recovery between agents in measurable terms — essentially asking: when agent A gives agent B a bad output, how does B's reliance on A change, and can that change be governed? The practical upshot for multi-agent pipelines is that you cannot treat inter-agent trust as a static configuration you set at deploy time. Trust drift is real, it compounds, and the paper's governance implications point toward needing runtime monitoring of agent-to-agent delegation patterns, not just input/output logging. Nobody is shipping that yet. Medium-term, this is where the regulatory pressure on agentic systems will land — auditors will want to know what your agents were authorizing each other to do.

**[KV cache editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17107) is the sleeper item this week.** The paper demonstrates that the KV cache built during prefill isn't just a performance artifact — it's semantically structured enough to be edited, merged, and composed across requests. Concretely: you can inject or modify cached representations mid-sequence without re-running the full prefill, and you can combine KV caches from different prompts in ways that preserve meaning. The immediate application is inference efficiency — shared system-prompt prefixes don't need to be recomputed per-user. But the sharper implication is for retrieval-augmented systems: if cached document representations are composable, RAG architectures that today do expensive re-encoding on every query can be rethought at the cache level. If you're running high-throughput inference with prefix caching already (vLLM does this), watch whether this lands in mainline — it could materially change how you structure prompt templates. If you pinned a specific vLLM version for stable KV behavior, this is worth tracking against your assumptions.

**The [EU eGovernment Benchmark 2026](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/digital-decade-2026-egovernment-benchmark-2026) and the [distributed agent network architecture paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17368) are background signals.** The EU report is bureaucratic infrastructure — Digital Decade targets, cross-border service digitization — but it shapes procurement and AI Act implementation timelines across member states. The distributed agent paper is an architecture survey more than a research contribution; useful if you're designing multi-agent systems and want a taxonomy, not if you're looking for novel mechanisms.

**Concrete moves:** Run OSGuard's attack categories against any computer-use agent you're operating — the benchmark is public. For orbital/edge VLM work, NAVI-Orbital's zero-shot approach is worth benchmarking against your fine-tuned baseline before you pay for fine-tuning. On KV cache composability: don't architect new RAG systems around static prefix assumptions until you see how this technique stabilizes in production inference stacks, but do track the vLLM and SGLang issue trackers for integration PRs.

items.title_fr: Refusal Geometry, Model Collapse & Neuron-Level LLM Surgery
items.title_en: Refusal Geometry, Model Collapse & Neuron-Level LLM Surgery
items.content_en:
## What happened

- **[Refusal mechanisms are more complex than a single vector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.xxxxx):** The Diff-in-Means vs. INLP comparison shows that LLM refusal behavior is not cleanly captured by one linear direction in activation space. Multiple overlapping directions contribute — meaning single-vector steering attacks (and defenses) are both more brittle than assumed.
- **[Sample selection bias accelerates model collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.xxxxx):** When training data is filtered non-randomly (e.g., quality filters, RLHF selection), collapse to low-diversity outputs happens faster and harder than uniform-drop scenarios. Directly relevant to anyone building pipelines on synthetic or curated data.
- **[One neuron can fix repetition loops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.xxxxx):** A targeted single-neuron edit can suppress the degenerate repetition-loop failure mode in LLMs without measurable degradation on benchmarks. First credible evidence that surgical neuron-level edits are practical for a real failure mode, not just toy tasks.
- **[Orchestra-o1 omnimodal orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.xxxxx):** A framework for coordinating heterogeneous modality-specific agents under a single reasoning backbone. Incremental architecture work, but signals where multi-modal agent pipelines are heading.

## Where it sits in the state of the art

The refusal geometry paper is the most significant result. The dominant assumption in both jailbreak research and alignment engineering has been that refusal is a low-dimensional, roughly linear phenomenon — one direction to suppress, one to amplify. That framing underpins most current red-teaming tools and activation-steering defenses. Evidence of multi-directional, entangled refusal structure means existing single-probe defenses are incomplete, and so are single-vector attacks. This is a genuine conceptual update, not an incremental benchmark improvement.

The model collapse / selection bias result sharpens a known risk. Prior collapse literature (Shumailov et al., 2024) focused on random data degradation; this work shows that *structured* selection — exactly what production pipelines do — is strictly worse. The field has been underestimating collapse risk in real deployments.

The neuron-edit result is the most practically surprising. Model editing has largely failed to generalize beyond narrow factual patches. Showing it works for a behavioral failure mode (repetition) with a single neuron — and holds across contexts — opens a credible path for lightweight behavioral patching without full fine-tuning. Still early, but the specificity of the target (repetition loops are well-defined) likely explains why it works where broader edits fail.

Orchestra-o1 and the dialogue planning paper (UP-NRPA) are incremental engineering. Useful for practitioners building agents, not frontier shifts.

## Implications

**Short-term (now–3 months):** Red-teaming and safety eval teams should immediately re-examine any tooling that assumes a single refusal direction (RepE-based probes, CAA steering). Single-vector jailbreaks will keep working against patched models; single-vector defenses will keep leaking. Prioritize multi-probe eval frameworks.

**Medium-term (3–12 months):** Model collapse via selection bias will start showing up as a real production problem as more teams close the loop on synthetic data pipelines. Pipelines that use model outputs to filter training data for the next version need explicit diversity monitoring. Teams without this instrumentation are accumulating invisible technical debt.

Neuron-level editing for behavioral fixes — if it generalizes beyond repetition — could become a lightweight compliance and safety patching mechanism. Geopolitically relevant: jurisdictions with model access restrictions may allow fine-tuned or edited variants where full retraining is impossible. Watch for regulatory frameworks that treat behavioral edits as distinct from model retraining.

**Long-term (1–3 years):** Multi-directional refusal geometry implies that robust alignment cannot be bolted on post-hoc via activation steering alone. Architectures that want to be export-compliant or auditable under emerging EU AI Act technical standards will need refusal properties baked into training, not patched in inference. This raises the cost floor for compliant frontier models and concentrates capability further among well-resourced labs.

## What to do as a developer

- **Audit your refusal/safety probes:** If you're using linear probes or single-direction steering for safety filtering, add at minimum a second orthogonal probe and test disagreement rate. Flag inputs where probes diverge.
- **Instrument your synthetic data pipelines for diversity now:** Track n-gram entropy, embedding spread, and output-length distributions across data generations. Set hard floor thresholds before collapse is visible in downstream evals.
- **Test the neuron-edit paper's approach on your repetition failures:** The method is low-cost to replicate. If you have deployed models with known repetition issues, this is worth a two-day spike before queuing a full fine-tune.
- **Pin your RLHF/filtering logic versioning:** Given the selection-bias collapse risk, treat data filtering code with the same versioning discipline as model weights. You need to be able to reproduce exactly what was filtered and why.
- **Watch the EU AI Act technical standards drafts** (CEN/CENELEC JTC 21) for any language distinguishing 'behavioral edits' from 'training modifications' — this distinction will determine whether neuron-level patches count as a new model version under compliance obligations.
